a search problem, explained from scratch

Coloring numbers without making a pattern

Here's a game. Color the numbers 1, 2, 3, and so on, using a few colors. One rule: you can't let a set of evenly-spaced numbers — like 3, 6, 9, 12 (each one 3 more than the last), or 5, 9, 13 (going up by 4) — all end up the same color. How far can you get?

That's the whole problem, and it's harder than it sounds. It's also an open question in math — the point where you're finally forced to break the rule is called a van der Waerden number, and for most sizes nobody knows it exactly. What pulled me in is that it's a search problem: you're looking for a coloring that follows the rule for as long as possible. I work in machine learning, where searching a huge space of options is the usual job, so my first instinct was to ask how you'd search this one efficiently. This is the story of why that instinct is wrong, and what works instead.

Part 1 — the game

Try it yourself

The evenly-spaced sets have a name: an arithmetic progression is a set of numbers with a constant gap, like 2, 5, 8, 11 (gap of 3). It's monochromatic when they're all the same color — that's the thing you're trying to avoid.

Below is a row of numbers. Click a cell to change its color. The strip watches for monochromatic progressions of the length set by the slider and outlines them. See how long you can keep it clean.

A run of one color is the obvious trap. The progressions with bigger gaps (heavy outline) are the ones that are easy to miss. More numbers and fewer colors make them harder to avoid — and van der Waerden's theorem says that past a certain point you can't avoid them at all. That cutoff is the number the field is after: W(r,t) is the smallest count of numbers that forces a monochromatic progression of length t when you have r colors. The records here are lower bounds on W(3,t) — colorings with 3 colors that make it a very long way without one.

Part 2 — the search

Too many colorings to search

A lower bound like W(3,25) > 23,265,537,145 is a specific claim: using 3 colors, you can get through the first 23 billion numbers with no monochromatic progression of length 25. To prove it, you need an actual coloring that does this. To find one by brute force, you'd check every possible coloring. There are too many.

At the real scale, N is about 23 billion and there are 323,000,000,000 colorings. That's a number with over 11 billion digits. There are about 10⁸⁰ atoms in the universe, which is 81 digits. Brute force isn't slow here. It's impossible.

Part 3 — the method

Build one coloring instead

So don't search. Build one coloring you can prove works.

The recipe uses clock arithmetic. Imagine a clock face with p hours on it, where p is a prime number. Whenever a count runs past p, it wraps back around, and you keep only where the hand lands. (For the math to work, p has to be one more than a multiple of 3 — like 7, 13, 31, or 61.)

To pick a number's color, multiply it by itself over and over — (p−1)/3 times — wrapping around the clock at each step. On a clock like this, that always lands on one of exactly three spots. Those three spots are the three colors. Every number sorts into one of them. You don't have to do any of this by hand; the strip below does it for whatever prime you pick. The one thing to hold onto is that the coloring is built out of multiplication — that is what makes it easy to check.

Pick a prime and look at the coloring it produces:

Why you only have to check for runs

Because the coloring is built out of multiplying, it has a symmetry. Take any evenly-spaced set of numbers and scale the whole thing — multiply every number in it by the same amount — so that it starts at 1. On the clock, multiplying everything by the same amount doesn't change any of their colors. So every evenly-spaced set is just a scaled copy of one that starts at 1.

That collapses the whole check down to looking for runs: stretches of numbers in a row that all came out the same color (the outlined stretch above). If the longest run is shorter than t, there's no monochromatic progression of length t anywhere. This is Rabung's criterion, and it's the reason this is cheap: instead of hunting through billions of progressions, you make one pass through the numbers and note the longest run. Do that once per prime. When the run comes up short, the coloring even repeats, tiling outward to cover the first (t−1)·p numbers.

Part 4 — the leverage

One test, not a billion

The run-check is the whole method, and it has a useful property. As the prime gets bigger, its longest run barely grows — it creeps up like a logarithm, only reaching about 19 even when the prime is near a billion. But the record you get out grows in lockstep with the prime itself:

W(3, t)  >  (t − 1) · p + 1   for every t past the longest run.

So one prime doesn't set one record. It sets every record for t past its longest run, all at once. A prime ten times bigger makes each of those records about ten times bigger. Move the prime out and the bounds move with it. Drag the slider:

Brute force asks which of the astronomically many colorings works, and there's no way to answer that. This method asks a different question — which primes have a short longest run — and that one you can answer, one prime at a time, with a single pass through the numbers. Near the frontier a lot of primes qualify. That's the whole thing.

Part 5 — the records

Seven records from one prime

The last exhaustive search stopped in 2019 at about p = 969,396,749. We scanned primes just past it. p = 969,397,381 has a longest run of 18. That one prime beats the previous best lower bound for every length from 19 to 25:

W(3, t)previous lower boundnew lower boundgain

Each record is (t−1) × 969,397,381 + 1. The previous values are Monroe's (JCMCC 128, Dec 2025). The whole certificate is just the prime plus the coloring rule. We double-checked each one two independent ways: a second, unrelated way of building the same coloring, and a slow, direct check for progressions at small scale.

Part 6 — check it

The certificate is one number

None of this asks you to trust us. To check W(3,25) > 23,265,537,145, rebuild the coloring of the numbers 1 through 969,397,380 using the clock-arithmetic rule from Part 3, scan it once for the longest run, and confirm it's under 25. That's the proof — the same pass this page runs on the small primes above, just with a bigger prime.

The code, the certificates, and the full notebook are in the repository. You can re-run it in an afternoon.

An explainer for the van der Waerden lower-bound work in this repo. The colors are a colorblind-safe set; class is also shown by the legend and by letters, not color alone. No libraries. Everything runs in your browser.
